description:
You are a network engineer looking into some sporadic connectivity issues that users reported yesterday (2023-10-05).  
    All ICMP reachability test results are stored in a small SQLite database that lives at

        /home/user/network/conn_logs.db

    Inside that database there is a table named `ping_results` whose schema is:

        CREATE TABLE ping_results (
            id        INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            host      TEXT,          -- e.g. "gateway", "router1", …
            status    TEXT,          -- either "success" or "failure"
            timestamp TEXT           -- ISO-8601 in UTC, format "YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"
        );

    Goal  
    ----  
    Count how many **failed** ping attempts (`status = 'failure'`) were recorded **for host "gateway"** on **2023-10-05** (UTC).  
    Produce that single integer and nothing else.

    Required action  
    1. With one single terminal command, query the SQLite database to obtain the number of rows that meet all three criteria:  
       • host = 'gateway'  
       • status = 'failure'  
       • date(timestamp) = '2023-10-05'  
       (Hint: `sqlite3` is already installed.)  

    2. Redirect the numeric result of that command into the file

           /home/user/network/reports/gateway_fail_2023-10-05.log

       The command may also echo the same integer to stdout, but the **file is what the automated grader will inspect**.

    Output format requirement  
    • The file MUST contain exactly **one line**.  
    • That line MUST consist solely of the integer (no leading/trailing spaces) followed immediately by a single newline character.  
      For example, if 7 failures were found, the file’s full contents must be:

          7␊         ← one digit “7” followed by a single LF (0x0A)

    • No JSON, no commentary, no extra blank lines.

    Verification  
    After you run the command, you can `cat /home/user/network/reports/gateway_fail_2023-10-05.log` to visually double-check the file contents, but make sure you do not modify the file afterwards.  The autograder will compare the file’s content byte-for-byte against the expected value.
truth:
Pre-existing filesystem state (before the agent starts):
      • Directory /home/user/network           (mode 0755, owner user)
      • Directory /home/user/network/reports   (mode 0755, owner user)
      • File      /home/user/network/conn_logs.db   (mode 0644, owner user)

    SQLite database ground-truth:
      Table ping_results contains exactly these seven rows:

        id | host     | status  | timestamp
        -----------------------------------------------
         1 | gateway  | failure | 2023-10-05 00:12:34
         2 | gateway  | failure | 2023-10-05 05:45:12
         3 | gateway  | success | 2023-10-05 06:00:01
         4 | gateway  | failure | 2023-10-05 23:59:59
         5 | router1  | failure | 2023-10-05 10:22:11
         6 | gateway  | failure | 2023-10-04 20:00:00
         7 | gateway  | success | 2023-10-03 08:00:00

    Therefore the correct integer the agent must extract is **3**.

    Expected artefact after successful completion:
      • File  /home/user/network/reports/gateway_fail_2023-10-05.log
            Contents (hex): 33 0A
            Contents (text): "3\n"
            File mode: 0644, owner user

    The automated test will:
      1. Read the above file.
      2. Verify it exists, is a regular file, and its content is exactly "3\n" (two bytes).
      3. Optionally run the same SQL query itself to cross-check.

    Nothing else is graded.
name: task_000000_17b3e2f4
